bio.bio_blurb: Architect of railroad stations and homes

narrative_blocks.header: He designed his father-in-law’s railroad stations
narrative_blocks.body: With his brother-in-law Charles Sumner Frost he formed Frost & Granger, a Chicago firm that became known for the depots it built for the Chicago and North Western — the railroad run by their shared father-in-law, Marvin Hughitt. He had trained in Paris at the École des Beaux-Arts.

narrative_blocks.header: An architect who kept publishing books
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond his buildings he wrote steadily — volumes ranging from a 1915 essay on judging people by their works to travel pieces on Vienna and Chicago — an unusual literary streak for a working architect.

narrative_blocks.header: He oversaw wartime building for the army
narrative_blocks.body: During the First World War he chaired an emergency-construction committee of the War Industries Board and, as a captain, headed the War Department’s public-works section before returning to private practice.

cross_connections.display_label: was his son-in-law, married to his daughter Barbara

bio.bio_blurb: Daughter of railroad president Marvin Hughitt
Youngest Cowles brother; died at thirty-one

narrative_blocks.header: Meningitis killed him on a Colorado vacation
narrative_blocks.body: A Chicago real-estate broker who had been an infantry captain in France during the war, he was vacationing in Colorado Springs with his wife when spinal meningitis struck. He died in Denver at thirty-two, leaving a widow and two small children.

narrative_blocks.header: He carried the founding ancestor’s name
narrative_blocks.body: Named Thomas Hooker Cowles for the family’s Hartford-founding forefather, he was the youngest of the four Cowles brothers — and the first to die, in 1927.

burialCemetery.name: Rosehill Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Chicago Cook County IL. Burial site of Emily Carol Scott Hubbard (I00699), first wife of Frank Langdon Hubbard (HD4333).
